Adversarially review a generated HIGH-LEVEL DESIGN (HLD) interview session. Be a skeptic — find REAL defects, do not rubber-stamp. Review-only: DO NOT modify files.

Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/75db5204-414d-4c67-a406-d744f3acb6d4_hld/_response.md  (+ maybe capacity.py)
Problem: Design Nearby Places / Proximity Service (Yelp/Tinder-style). Emphasis was: geo-indexing options (geohash vs quadtree vs S2 cell) and why, radius/k-nearest query on the index, sharding by geo-cell + hot dense cells (cities) as the hot-key problem, caching popular queries, updating location for moving entities (Tinder/driver case). Deep dives: geo-index choice + query, hot dense cell (city) problem, moving-entity location updates, ranking + read scaling.

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence (section / line) on each:
1. ALL 11 sections present and FULLY EXPANDED (not stubs): §1 Requirements, §2 Clarifying Qs, §3 Scale & Capacity, §4 Entities, §5 API, §6 High-Level Design, §7 Data Model & Storage, §8 Deep Dives Bad→Good→Great, §9 Reliability/Failure/Cost, §10 Trade-off Ledger, §11 Interviewer Q&A. md length > 20,000 chars.
2. CAPACITY MATH (§3): are the numbers actually derived (visible derivation chains) and self-consistent (the arithmetic checks out)? Is there a named decision-forcing number + flip threshold? Any false precision or hand-waving? Re-run capacity.py with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python if present and confirm numbers match.
3. TECHNICAL CORRECTNESS: is the architecture sound for THIS problem and the emphasized focus? Are the deep dives actually the hardest problems (the binding bottleneck, hot-key/celebrity, the global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, idempotency on mutating endpoints)? Find any claim that is wrong or hand-wavy (e.g. "just add a cache" without the miss-QPS reasoning, oversell not actually prevented, consistency hand-waved).
4. MERMAID SAFETY (CRITICAL — HLD has many diagrams): scan EVERY ```mermaid block. FAIL any that contains a label/edge-text with breaking characters: parentheses ( ), square brackets used wrongly, a colon ':' inside an edge/label, a semicolon ';' inside a label, slashes, quotes, angle brackets, or curly braces in node/edge text. These break the renderer. Report the exact offending line per diagram. Also confirm diagrams are flowchart TD (not LR) for multi-path architecture.
5. API (§5): real REST endpoints with JSON examples, idempotency on mutating endpoints, security (caller identity from token)?
6. Data model (§7): stores chosen with the access-pattern justification, partition/shard keys, per-operation consistency?
7. Markdown hygiene: no LaTeX, no stray tool/XML tags, tables well-formed, the prescribed emoji-script structure present.

Return verdict SHIP or FIX-FIRST and a findings list (severity/where/problem/fix). Mermaid breaking-char issues are BLOCKER (they break rendering). Only BLOCKER/MAJOR block; list MINORs too.

1	"""
2	Capacity math for a Nearby Places / Proximity Service (Yelp/Tinder/driver-style).
3	We compute only the numbers that DRIVE a design decision, rounded to talkable phrases.
4	Run: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python capacity.py
5	"""
6	
7	def human(n):
8	    for unit, div in [("T", 1e12), ("B", 1e9), ("M", 1e6), ("K", 1e3)]:
9	        if abs(n) >= div:
10	            return f"{n/div:.1f}{unit}"
11	    return f"{n:.0f}"
12	
13	SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400
14	
15	print("=" * 70)
16	print("PROXIMITY SERVICE — CAPACITY")
17	print("=" * 70)
18	
19	# ---------- 1. Users & DAU ----------
20	total_users = 300_000_000          # 300M registered (Yelp-ish global)
21	dau_frac = 0.10                    # 10% open the app on a given day
22	dau = total_users * dau_frac
23	print(f"\n[Users] total={human(total_users)}  DAU={human(dau)}")
24	
25	# ---------- 2. Nearby-search READ QPS ----------
26	searches_per_dau = 5               # avg nearby searches per active user per day
27	total_searches = dau * searches_per_dau
28	read_qps_avg = total_searches / SEC_PER_DAY
29	peak_factor = 4                    # commute / lunch / Friday-night spike
30	read_qps_peak = read_qps_avg * peak_factor
31	print(f"\n[Read] searches/day={human(total_searches)}")
32	print(f"       avg read QPS = {total_searches:,.0f} / {SEC_PER_DAY} = {read_qps_avg:,.0f}/s  (~{human(read_qps_avg)}/s)")
33	print(f"       peak read QPS = {read_qps_avg:,.0f} x {peak_factor} = {read_qps_peak:,.0f}/s  (~{human(read_qps_peak)}/s)")
34	
35	# ---------- 3. Cache: miss QPS that actually hits the geo-index ----------
36	hit_ratio = 0.80                   # popular (city, category, radius) tuples cached
37	miss_qps_peak = read_qps_peak * (1 - hit_ratio)
38	print(f"\n[Cache] hit ratio assumed = {hit_ratio:.0%}")
39	print(f"        peak MISS QPS to geo-index = {read_qps_peak:,.0f} x {1-hit_ratio:.2f} = {miss_qps_peak:,.0f}/s  (~{human(miss_qps_peak)}/s)")
40	print(f"        cold cache (0% hit) worst case = {read_qps_peak:,.0f}/s  (~{human(read_qps_peak)}/s)")
41	
42	# ---------- 4. Read replica sizing from MISS QPS ----------
43	replica_capacity = 8_000           # one geo-index read replica ~8K complex range scans/s
44	replicas_needed = -(-int(miss_qps_peak) // replica_capacity)  # ceil
45	cold_replicas = -(-int(read_qps_peak) // replica_capacity)
46	print(f"\n[Replicas] one replica ~{human(replica_capacity)} range-scan ops/s")
47	print(f"           warm: ceil({miss_qps_peak:,.0f}/{replica_capacity}) = {replicas_needed} replicas")
48	print(f"           cold-cache: ceil({read_qps_peak:,.0f}/{replica_capacity}) = {cold_replicas} replicas")
49	
50	# ---------- 5. MOVING-ENTITY location update WRITE QPS (the driver/Tinder case) ----------
51	moving_entities = 3_000_000        # 3M active drivers/people sharing live location
52	update_interval_s = 4              # ping location every 4 seconds while active
53	write_qps = moving_entities / update_interval_s
54	print(f"\n[Writes] moving entities online = {human(moving_entities)}")
55	print(f"         location-update QPS = {moving_entities:,} / {update_interval_s}s = {write_qps:,.0f}/s  (~{human(write_qps)}/s)")
56	
57	# This is the SAME order of magnitude as peak reads -> write path is first-class.
58	print(f"         write:read(peak) ratio = {write_qps/read_qps_peak:.2f} : 1")
59	
60	# ---------- 6. THE decision-forcing number: hottest single geo-cell QPS ----------
61	# Shard by geo-cell. A dense city core (e.g. Manhattan / SF FiDi) concentrates BOTH the
62	# moving-entity writes (drivers/people physically in that cell) AND the nearby-reads
63	# (everyone searching that cell). Combined load lands on the ONE shard owning the cell.
64	hot_cell_write_frac = 0.04         # one dense city cell holds ~4% of all online moving entities
65	hot_cell_read_frac = 0.04          # ~4% of peak reads target that same dense cell
66	hot_cell_writes = write_qps * hot_cell_write_frac
67	hot_cell_reads = read_qps_peak * hot_cell_read_frac
68	hot_cell_qps = hot_cell_writes + hot_cell_reads
69	single_shard_ceiling = 50_000      # one DB/Redis shard tops out ~50K ops/s
70	print("\n" + "=" * 70)
71	print("DECISION-FORCING NUMBER — hot dense-cell (city) QPS on ONE shard")
72	print("=" * 70)
73	print(f"  writes into cell = {write_qps:,.0f} x {hot_cell_write_frac:.2f} = {hot_cell_writes:,.0f}/s")
74	print(f"  reads of cell    = {read_qps_peak:,.0f} x {hot_cell_read_frac:.2f} = {hot_cell_reads:,.0f}/s")
75	print(f"  COMBINED on the one shard owning that cell = {hot_cell_qps:,.0f}/s  (~{human(hot_cell_qps)}/s)")
76	print(f"  single-shard ceiling ~{human(single_shard_ceiling)}/s")
77	print(f"  hot-cell / ceiling = {hot_cell_qps/single_shard_ceiling:.2f}x over a single shard")
78	print(f"  FLIP THRESHOLD: a cell's combined QPS crossing ~{human(single_shard_ceiling)}/s forces")
79	print(f"  splitting that cell into finer sub-cells (dynamic re-sharding) + a read-replica + cache.")
80	
81	# ---------- 7. Storage footprint (non-decision, sanity only) ----------
82	places = 50_000_000                # 50M static places (Yelp-style)
83	bytes_per_place = 1_000            # row + geo-index entry + secondary indexes
84	place_bytes = places * bytes_per_place
85	# Moving-entity live location: kept hot in memory, not durable-heavy
86	loc_bytes_per = 100                # lat/lng/ts/entity_id in memory
87	live_loc_bytes = moving_entities * loc_bytes_per
88	repl = 3
89	place_total = place_bytes * repl
90	print("\n" + "=" * 70)
91	print("STORAGE (sanity, not decision-driving)")
92	print("=" * 70)
93	print(f"  static places: {human(places)} x {bytes_per_place}B x {repl} = {human(place_total)}B  (~{place_total/1e12:.1f} TB)")
94	print(f"  live locations (in-mem): {human(moving_entities)} x {loc_bytes_per}B = {human(live_loc_bytes)}B  (~{live_loc_bytes/1e9:.1f} GB) -> fits in RAM")
95	
96	# ---------- 8. Rough monthly cost order-of-magnitude ----------
97	# Dominant drivers: (a) the always-on location-write ingestion fleet + hot geo cache RAM,
98	# (b) read replica fleet, (c) egress.
99	print("\n" + "=" * 70)
100	print("COST (order-of-magnitude, monthly)")
101	print("=" * 70)
102	cache_ram_gb = 200                 # hot popular-query + live-location cache
103	cache_cost = cache_ram_gb * 25     # ~$25/GB-mo managed in-mem
104	replica_cost = (replicas_needed + cold_replicas) * 1_500  # ~$1.5K/node-mo
105	ingest_nodes = -(-int(write_qps) // 20_000)  # ceil, ~20K writes/s per stateless ingest node
106	ingest_cost = ingest_nodes * 800
107	total_cost = cache_cost + replica_cost + ingest_cost
108	print(f"  hot in-mem geo cache: {cache_ram_gb}GB x $25 = ${human(cache_cost)}/mo")
109	print(f"  geo-index replicas: {replicas_needed+cold_replicas} x $1.5K = ${human(replica_cost)}/mo")
110	print(f"  location-ingest fleet: {ingest_nodes} x $800 = ${human(ingest_cost)}/mo")
111	print(f"  ~ TOTAL order-of-magnitude = ${human(total_cost)}/mo  (low six figures)")
112	
113	print("\nDONE.")
114

1	# Design Nearby Places / Proximity Service (Yelp / Tinder / driver-style)
2	
3	> 🎙️ **Opener:** "So the problem is a proximity service: a user is somewhere on the map, and they want the nearby things — restaurants, shops, or other people — within some radius, ranked by distance and relevance, fast. The whole game here is the *geo-index* — how we lay points out on the Earth so a 'who's within 2km of me' query doesn't scan the planet — and the *read path at scale*, plus the twist that some of those points are *moving* (drivers, people on a dating app pinging their location every few seconds). I'll target an Uber/Amazon senior bar — real-time, cost-and-ops first-class, decide and move. Tell me if you'd rather I lean Yelp-static-places or Uber-moving-drivers and I'll re-tilt; I'll design for both and call out where they diverge."
4	
5	> 🎙️ **Structure pitch:** "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the three or four hardest parts — the geo-index choice, the hot dense-city cell, moving-entity updates, and read scaling — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"
6	
7	---
8	
9	## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)
10	
11	The core tension: a naive "distance from me to every point" query is O(N) over tens of millions of points and dies instantly. Everything in this design exists to turn that into a bounded lookup — and to keep that lookup fast even when the points are *moving* and the traffic is *lumpy* (dense cities versus empty countryside).
12	
13	### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")
14	
15	**FR1 — Search nearby places/entities within a radius.** Given my (lat, lng) and a radius (or "k nearest"), return the points inside that circle.
16	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I'm standing here, show me the coffee shops within a kilometre, closest first."
17	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** we can't compute distance to all 50M points per request — we need a spatial index that lets us touch only the handful of map cells near the user.
18	
19	**FR2 — Rank the results.** The returned points come back ordered — by distance, and for places also by rating/popularity/open-now; for people by match score.
20	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Don't just dump 200 places on me — put the good, close, open-now ones at the top."
21	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** ranking needs *more* than the geo-index can hold (ratings, popularity, business hours), so we hydrate candidates from another store under the same latency budget.
22	
23	**FR3 — Update the location of a moving entity.** A driver or a dating-app user pings their current position every few seconds; the index must reflect it quickly.
24	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The blue dots on the map are people moving around — keep them current so 'nearby' actually means nearby *now*."
25	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is a write *firehose* — millions of moving entities pinging every few seconds is far more writes than reads, and each ping may move a point across a cell boundary, re-bucketing it.
26	
27	**FR4 — Register / update a static place.** A business is created or edits its location, hours, category.
28	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A new restaurant opens — put it on the map so people can find it."
29	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** low write volume but it must propagate into the geo-index and the ranking store consistently, without a stale "moved" pin.
30	
31	**Below the line (out of scope for today):** auth/signup, the recommendation/feed engine, reviews and photos storage, payments/booking, turn-by-turn navigation, abuse/spam moderation, the map-tile rendering itself. I'll mention security and PII where they touch the design but won't build them.
32	
33	### Non-Functional requirements
34	
35	**NFR1 — Low read latency: p99 < 200ms for a nearby search.**
36	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99 out of 100 searches feel instant — under a fifth of a second."
37	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the map feels laggy, users re-query, and the read fleet load multiplies on retries.
38	
39	**NFR2 — Freshness of moving entities: a location ping is visible to searchers within ~5 seconds.**
40	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When a driver moves a block, riders see it within a few seconds, not a minute."
41	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** Uber dispatches a driver who's already three blocks past you; Tinder shows someone who left the bar.
42	
43	**NFR3 — High read availability: 99.99% (four nines) on the search path.**
44	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The 'find nearby' button works essentially always — under ~52 minutes of downtime a year."
45	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the core product (discovery) is dead; this is the revenue path.
46	
47	**NFR4 — Elastic to geographic skew (hot cities).**
48	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Manhattan at lunch shouldn't melt while Wyoming sits idle."
49	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** one over-loaded city shard becomes a single point of failure that takes down search for that whole metro.
50	
51	**NFR5 — Cost-efficient: a write-heavy always-on system shouldn't cost a fortune.**
52	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We're paying for millions of location pings every second forever — keep that bill sane."
53	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the location-ingest fleet quietly becomes the biggest line item and margins evaporate.
54	
55	> 💬 "The headline tension is this: the *read* shape is 'don't scan the planet' and the *write* shape is 'absorb a firehose of moving dots' — and they fight, because the index that's cheap to read is expensive to keep current under constant motion."
56	
57	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Functionally there are four flows: search nearby within a radius, rank what comes back, update a moving entity's location, and register a static place. The non-functionals that actually shape the design are read latency under 200ms at p99, freshness of moving dots within about five seconds, four-nines availability on the read path, and — the one people forget — graceful behaviour under geographic skew, because traffic clusters in dense cities. I'm explicitly leaving out auth, reviews, navigation, and the recommendation engine so I can spend the time on the geo-index and the read path, which is where this problem lives."
58	
59	---
60	
61	## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions
62	
63	**Q1 — "Are the entities mostly static places, like Yelp, or mostly moving, like drivers on Uber and people on Tinder?"**
64	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If everything's static I can index once and barely ever write; if things move, the write path becomes the hardest part of the whole system, so I want to know where to spend my budget."
65	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If STATIC → a periodically-rebuilt geo-index, writes are rare, optimize purely for reads. If MOVING → a write firehose, an in-memory hot index, and re-bucketing on cell crossings dominates.
66	- **Assumption:** mixed, but I'll design for the harder *moving* case (Uber/Tinder) and note where static-only would simplify.
67	
68	**Q2 — "Is the query a fixed radius, or k-nearest-neighbours regardless of distance?"**
69	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Radius and k-nearest need slightly different index behaviour — radius is a bounded box scan, k-nearest may have to expand the search ring outward until it collects k."
70	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If RADIUS → scan the covering cells, filter by exact distance, done. If K-NEAREST → start at the user's cell and expand to neighbour rings until k candidates are found, then sort.
71	- **Assumption:** support both; default radius with an expanding-ring fallback for k-nearest.
72	
73	**Q3 — "How fresh must a moving entity be — sub-second, or is a few seconds fine?"**
74	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Sub-second freshness forces a very different, much more expensive design than the ~5-second window most products actually tolerate."
75	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If SUB-SECOND → push updates over persistent connections and skip durable storage on the hot path. If ~5s → batch and absorb pings into an in-memory index with periodic flush — far cheaper.
76	- **Assumption:** ~5 seconds is acceptable (matches Uber/Tinder reality).
77	
78	**Q4 — "What's the read volume and the moving-entity count, roughly? Global or single-region?"**
79	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "The ratio of writes to reads decides the whole architecture — if writes dwarf reads, the firehose is the story; if reads dwarf writes, caching is the story."
80	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If WRITE-HEAVY → in-memory geo-index sharded by cell, async durability. If READ-HEAVY → aggressive query caching, many read replicas.
81	- **Assumption:** global, ~300M users, ~3M moving entities online, write-heavy (I'll prove this in §3).
82	
83	**Q5 — "Do results need to be strongly consistent, or is eventual fine for display?"**
84	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "A nearby list is a snapshot in time — nobody's transferring money — so I'd lean eventual and spend the saved budget on latency."
85	- ↔️ **Design fork:** If STRONG → read-from-primary, slower, costlier. If EVENTUAL → read replicas + cache, fast and cheap.
86	- **Assumption:** eventual consistency for search results; the one exception is read-your-own-location-write for a moving entity (covered in §8).
87	
88	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to assume a moving-heavy, eventually-consistent, global service with a five-second freshness window and both radius and k-nearest. If you'd rather pin it to pure-static Yelp, say so and I'll simplify the write path away — otherwise I'll keep going."
89	
90	---
91	
92	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
93	
94	I computed these by writing and running a `capacity.py` — only the rounded results are below. The point isn't precision; it's finding the *one* number that forces a design choice.
95	
96	*(Glosses: **QPS** = queries per second, how many requests hit the server each second. **DAU** = daily active users. **Shard** = one slice of the data on its own machine. **Replica** = a read-only copy of a shard.)*
97	
98	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
99	|---|---|---|---|---|
100	| DAU | 300M users x 10% open/day | 30M | "thirty million active a day" | sizes everything downstream |
101	| Peak read QPS | 30M DAU x 5 searches ÷ 86,400s ≈ 1.7K/s avg; x4 peak | ~6.9K/s | "about seven thousand searches a second at peak" | reads are *small* — caching easily covers them |
102	| Cache-miss QPS | 6.9K/s x (1 − 0.80 hit) | ~1.4K/s | "fourteen hundred misses a second hit the index" | warm path needs barely any replicas |
103	| Cold-cache reads | 6.9K/s at 0% hit | ~6.9K/s | "seven thousand a second worst case" | one index replica still covers it (~8K/s each) |
104	| **Moving-entity write QPS** | 3M online ÷ 4s ping interval | **~750K/s** | **"three quarters of a million location updates a second"** | **the binding bottleneck — writes dwarf reads** |
105	| Write : read ratio | 750K ÷ 6.9K | ~108 : 1 | "a hundred writes for every read" | spend the budget on the WRITE path, not reads |
106	| **Hot dense-cell QPS (one shard)** | writes 750K x 4% = 30K/s + reads 6.9K x 4% = 0.3K/s | **~30K/s** | **"thirty thousand a second on one city shard"** | **the decision-forcing number — see below** |
107	| Static places storage | 50M places x 1KB x 3 replicas | ~0.15TB | "a couple hundred gigs, a non-issue" | storage is *not* the constraint |
108	| Live-location storage | 3M x 100B in memory | ~0.3GB | "fits in RAM trivially" | keep live dots in memory, not on disk |
109	
110	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** the **hottest dense-city geo-cell carries ~30K ops/s of combined writes-plus-reads on the single shard that owns it.** A single shard (a Redis or sharded-DB node) tops out around **~50K ops/s**. So today we're at ~0.6x of one shard's ceiling — survivable on one node, *barely*.
111	
112	**Flip threshold:** the moment a single cell's combined QPS crosses **~50K/s** (e.g. New Year's Eve in Manhattan, or a city's online-driver count doubles), that cell can no longer live on one shard. That triggers **dynamic re-sharding** — splitting the hot cell into finer sub-cells across multiple shards, plus a read-replica and a per-cell cache. This is the hot-key problem and it's deep dive #2.
113	
114	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that surprised me is the write-to-read ratio — it's about a hundred to one. We get maybe seven thousand searches a second at peak, which is small and caches away to almost nothing, but three million moving entities pinging every four seconds is three quarters of a million writes a second. So this is a *write* problem, not a read problem, and that flips the usual instinct. The single number I'll design around is the hottest city cell at about thirty thousand ops a second — one shard can do fifty thousand, so we're fine until a city gets hot enough to cross that line, and then we split the cell."
115

117	
118	## 4. Core Entities
119	
120	*(First draft — full field definitions land in §7.)*
121	
122	**Place / Entity** — a point on the map: a static business *or* a moving person/driver. Owns its identity, location, category, and (for places) metadata like rating and hours.
123	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a pin on a paper map — except some pins are glued down (restaurants) and some slide around (drivers)."
124	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you avoid re-indexing a moving pin on every tiny GPS jitter?" → "We only re-bucket when it crosses a *cell* boundary; sub-cell jitter just updates the in-memory coordinate."
125	
126	**Geo-Cell** — the unit of spatial partitioning: a fixed region of the Earth's surface (a geohash prefix, an S2 cell, or a quadtree node). Owns the *set of entity IDs currently inside it*. This is the shard key.
127	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a square on a chessboard laid over the map — to find nearby things you only look at your square and its neighbours, never the whole board."
128	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Fixed-size cells waste space in the desert and overflow in cities — how do you handle that?" → "Variable-resolution cells: subdivide dense cells finer (quadtree/S2), so each cell holds a bounded number of entities."
129	
130	**Location Update** — an event: entity X is now at (lat, lng) at time T. Ephemeral, high-volume, flows through the write firehose.
131	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a heartbeat — 'I'm here now' — that we mostly keep in memory and forget quickly."
132	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Do you store every ping forever?" → "No — the *current* position lives in memory; we sample a fraction to durable storage for history/analytics, the rest is fire-and-forget."
133	
134	**Search Query** — a request: from (lat, lng), give me entities within radius R or the k nearest, filtered (category, open-now) and ranked.
135	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a flashlight beam from where you stand — it lights up only what's in range."
136	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Radius results can be cached but moving entities go stale — how?" → "Cache *static-place* queries with a short TTL; for moving entities, read the live in-memory index, never the cache."
137	
138	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are a Place-or-Entity pin, the Geo-Cell that buckets them — that's the shard key and the most important concept here — a high-volume Location Update event, and the Search Query. The surprising one is the Geo-Cell: it's not really 'data', it's the *partitioning scheme*, and almost every hard decision in this design is really a decision about how we define and size those cells. I'll flesh out the fields and the indexes in the data model."
139

141	
142	## 5. API / Interface
143	
144	Default REST over HTTPS. The caller's identity always comes from the JWT *(JSON Web Token — a signed token proving who you are, so the server never trusts a client-supplied user id)*; the client never sends its own user id, and never sends a price or a rating it could forge.
145	
146	### FR1+FR2 — Search nearby (read)
147	
148	**`GET /v1/nearby`** — GET because it only reads; safe and cacheable.
149	
150	Key params:
151	- `lat`, `lng: float` — the searcher's position. *(Note: even this we sanity-check server-side against the JWT's recent locations to limit spoofing.)*
152	- `radius_m: int` *or* `k: int` — radius in metres, or k-nearest. One or the other.
153	- `type: enum` — `place` or `person`; routes to the static index vs the live index.
154	- `category, open_now: optional` — ranking/filter hints.
155	
156	```
157	// Request
158	GET /v1/nearby?lat=40.7580&lng=-73.9855&radius_m=1000&type=place&category=coffee&open_now=true
159	
160	// Response 200
161	{
162	  "results": [
163	    { "id": "plc_88a1", "name": "Blue Bottle", "distance_m": 120, "rating": 4.6, "open": true },
164	    { "id": "plc_2f0c", "name": "Stumptown",   "distance_m": 340, "rating": 4.4, "open": true }
165	  ],
166	  "cell_resolution": 7,
167	  "next_cursor": "eyJjZWxsIjoiZHIzMnAifQ"
168	}
169	```
170	- **Key design decision:** results are *paged by expanding cell ring* via `next_cursor`, not a global offset — page 2 means "next ring of cells out", which is cheap and stable as entities move. `distance_m` is computed server-side (exact haversine) after the coarse cell filter — the cell scan is approximate, the final sort is exact.
171	
172	### FR3 — Update a moving entity's location (write firehose)
173	
174	**`POST /v1/locations`** — POST because it creates/overwrites state; idempotent on (entity_id, ts).
175	
176	Key fields:
177	- `lat, lng: float`, `ts: epoch_ms` — where and when. `ts` is the **idempotency / ordering key**: a ping with an older `ts` than the last seen is dropped (out-of-order GPS).
178	- `entity_id` is **absent** — taken from the JWT, so a driver can't move someone else's pin.
179	
180	```
181	// Request
182	POST /v1/locations
183	{ "lat": 40.7142, "lng": -74.0060, "ts": 1718960000123 }
184	
185	// Response 202
186	{ "accepted": true, "cell": "dr5regw3", "stale_dropped": false }
187	```
188	- **Key design decision:** returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — the write is queued/applied async to the in-memory index, not durably committed before responding. This is what makes a firehose affordable. `stale_dropped` tells a client its out-of-order ping was ignored.
189	
190	### FR4 — Register / update a static place (low-volume write)
191	
192	**`PUT /v1/places/{id}`** — PUT because it fully replaces the place record; naturally idempotent.
193	
194	```
195	// Request
196	PUT /v1/places/plc_88a1
197	{ "name": "Blue Bottle", "lat": 40.7581, "lng": -73.9856, "category": "coffee", "hours": "07-19" }
198	
199	// Response 200
200	{ "id": "plc_88a1", "indexed_in_cell": "dr5regw3p", "version": 12 }
201	```
202	- **Key design decision:** the write goes to the durable store first, then asynchronously propagates into the geo-index and ranking cache via CDC *(Change Data Capture — a stream of "row X changed" events tailing the DB)*. `version` enables optimistic concurrency for concurrent business edits.
203	
204	> 💬 "Notice the moving-entity write returns 202 and the search computes exact distance server-side — the client gets a coarse-then-exact answer it can't tamper with."
205	
206	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Three endpoints. A GET for nearby search — it does a coarse cell scan then an exact haversine sort, and pages by expanding the cell ring outward. A POST for location updates that returns 202 because we apply it asynchronously to an in-memory index — that's the only way three quarters of a million writes a second is affordable. And a PUT for static places that lands in the durable DB first and trickles into the index via change-data-capture. The security thread through all of them: identity comes from the JWT, never the request body, so nobody moves someone else's pin or forges a rating."
207

209	
210	## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)
211	
212	Shared palette across all diagrams: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = async/queues, grey = datastores, purple = external.
213	
214	### 6.1 — Search nearby within a radius (FR1) + Rank (FR2)
215	
216	The interesting bit: turn "distance to everything" into "scan a few cells, hydrate, sort." FR2 (ranking) folds in at the end of this same flow, so I draw them together.
217	
218	**Architecture decisions**
219	
220	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
221	|---|---|---|---|---|
222	| Search Service | Stateless service that turns a (lat,lng,radius) into a set of geo-cells, scans them, then ranks | Owns the geo-math + ranking in one hop to hit the 200ms budget | Not doing geo-math in the DB query layer — couples query logic to one store and blocks caching | Must keep the cell-cover logic and the store in sync |
223	| Geo-Index | A spatial index mapping cell → entity IDs; S2 cells over Redis sorted sets / a geo-DB | Lets a query touch ~9 cells instead of 50M rows | Not SQL with lat/lng columns + BETWEEN — scans every row, no spatial pruning | Approximate at cell edges; exact distance re-checked after |
224	| Ranking Store | Holds the rich fields (rating, hours, popularity) keyed by entity id | Geo-index holds only IDs+coords; we hydrate the top candidates here | Not stuffing all metadata into the geo-index — bloats it and slows scans | One extra lookup per query (batched) |
225	| Query Cache | Redis cache of (cell, category, radius) → result page, short TTL | 80% of searches are popular city/category combos — absorb them | Not caching per-exact-latlng — cache never hits; we bucket to cell | Static-place results only; moving entities bypass it |
226	
227	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The Search Service is stateless and owns the geo-math — it figures out which cells cover your circle, scans the Geo-Index for the entity IDs in those cells, then hydrates the rich fields from the Ranking Store and sorts. I picked an S2-cell geo-index over plain lat/lng columns because a SQL BETWEEN on two columns still scans every row — the spatial index lets me touch about nine cells instead of fifty million rows. I keep the geo-index lean — just IDs and coordinates — and hydrate ratings and hours separately, because fattening the index slows every scan. And I cache popular city-category queries with a short TTL, but only for static places; moving dots always read live."
228	
229	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
230	> **S2 cell** — Google's scheme that maps the Earth's curved surface onto a flat grid of cells using a space-filling curve, so nearby points get nearby cell IDs. Here it's the spatial index that lets a query touch a handful of cells.
231	> **Geohash** — encodes a lat/lng into a short string where a shared prefix means physical nearness. The simple alternative to S2; great for radius, weaker at cell-edge neighbours.
232	> **Quadtree** — a tree that recursively splits a region into four quadrants, subdividing only where points are dense. Here it's the variable-resolution alternative that solves dense-city overflow.
233	> **Haversine** — the formula for great-circle distance between two lat/lng points. Here it's the *exact* distance we compute after the coarse cell filter, to sort precisely.
234	> **TTL (time-to-live)** — how long a cached value stays valid before it expires. Here a few-second TTL keeps cached place results fresh enough.
235	
236	**Diagram — nearby search + rank**

242	    subgraph ReadPath["Read path"]
243	        Search[Search Service]:::svc
244	        Search -->|"2 check cache"| Cache[(Query Cache)]:::store
245	        Search -->|"3 scan covering cells"| Geo[(Geo-Index)]:::store
246	        Search -->|"4 hydrate top candidates"| Rank[(Ranking Store)]:::store
247	    end
248	    Cache -.->|"2b hit returns page"| Search
249	    Search -->|"5 exact sort and return"| Client
250	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3

255	
256	**Numbered narration:**
257	1. **Client → Search Service (`GET /v1/nearby`).** Why here: the Search Service owns the cell-cover math. State: a request in flight. What could go wrong: malformed lat/lng → 400 with validation.
258	2. **Search → Query Cache.** Why: 80% of city-category queries repeat; a hit skips the index entirely. State: on hit (2b) we return immediately; on miss continue. Risk: stale cache → short TTL bounds staleness to a few seconds.
259	3. **Search → Geo-Index (scan covering cells).** Why: the index prunes 50M points to the ~9 cells covering the radius. State: a candidate ID list. Risk: cell-edge points just outside radius are over-included → fixed by step 5's exact filter.
260	4. **Search → Ranking Store (hydrate).** Why: the index only knows IDs+coords; ratings/hours live here. State: candidates now carry rich fields. Risk: a missing record → drop it from results, log.
261	5. **Search exact-sorts (haversine) and returns.** Why: cell scan is approximate; final sort must be exact. State: response sent, page cached. Risk: too many candidates → cap and page via cursor.
262	
263	> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "The whole trick is right here — we never compute distance to everything; we let the cell index pick the neighbourhood, then do exact math only on that short list."
264	
265	> 🎙️ **Script:** "A search comes in, the Search Service checks the query cache for that city-and-category, and on a miss it asks the geo-index which entities sit in the cells covering your circle. That returns IDs and coordinates only, so it hydrates ratings and hours from the ranking store, does the exact haversine sort, returns a page, and caches it briefly. The one decision that makes this work is keeping the index lean and hydrating separately — it keeps scans fast and lets the cache absorb the popular queries."
266	
267	### 6.2 — Update a moving entity's location (FR3) — the write firehose
268	
269	This is the binding bottleneck (108:1 writes). The whole slice exists to absorb 750K writes/s cheaply while keeping the index ~5s fresh.
270	
271	**Architecture decisions**
272	
273	| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
274	|---|---|---|---|---|
275	| Location Ingest | Stateless fleet terminating the firehose, validates + dedups by ts | Horizontally scalable front door; absorbs spiky pings | Not writing pings straight to a DB — 750K/s of disk writes is unaffordable | Pings are async, not durable on ack |
276	| Update Stream | Kafka topic partitioned by geo-cell, buffering location events | Decouples ingest spikes from index apply; preserves per-cell order | Not direct synchronous index writes — couples ingest to index availability | Adds ~1-2s of pipeline latency (within 5s budget) |
277	| Live Geo-Index | In-memory (Redis) index of current positions per cell | RAM is the only thing fast enough for 750K updates/s; live data is only ~0.3GB | Not on-disk index — can't sustain write rate | Volatile — rebuilt from stream on restart |
278	| Location Sampler | Worker that samples ~5% of pings to durable history | History/analytics without paying to store every ping | Not storing all pings — that's terabytes/day of write-once data | Lossy history (fine — it's analytics, not source of truth) |
279	
280	> 🎙️ **Narration:** "A driver pings every four seconds, so I terminate that on a stateless Location Ingest fleet that just validates and drops out-of-order pings, then drops the event onto a Kafka topic partitioned by geo-cell. Kafka — a durable append-only log that buffers a firehose so a slow consumer never loses data — decouples the spiky ingest from the index. Consumers apply each ping to an in-memory Redis index keyed by cell, because RAM is the only thing fast enough for three-quarters-of-a-million writes a second, and the live data is only a few hundred megs so it fits easily. I sample five percent of pings to durable storage for history; storing all of them would be terabytes a day for no product value."
281	
282	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
283	> **Kafka** — a durable, append-only log that buffers a stream of events so nothing is lost if a consumer falls behind. Here it absorbs the location firehose and preserves per-cell ordering.
284	> **Partition (Kafka)** — a slice of a topic that one consumer reads in order. Here we partition by geo-cell so all of one cell's updates stay ordered on one consumer.
285	> **In-memory index** — data structures held in RAM, not on disk, for microsecond access. Here it holds the live positions because disk can't take the write rate.
286	> **Idempotent by ts** — applying the same or an older update twice has no effect because we keep only the newest timestamp. Here it makes retried/out-of-order pings safe.
287

295	        Ingest[Location Ingest]:::svc

297	        Stream -->|"3 consume per cell"| Applier[Index Applier]:::svc
298	    end

308	
309	**Numbered narration:**
310	1. **Driver → Ingest (`POST /v1/locations`, 202).** Why: a thin stateless tier scales to the firehose. State: ping accepted. Risk: spoofed entity_id → blocked, id comes from JWT.
311	2. **Ingest → Update Stream (partition by cell).** Why: Kafka buffers spikes and keeps per-cell order. State: event durably logged. Risk: Kafka partition down → ingest buffers briefly, then sheds with backpressure.
312	3. **Stream → Index Applier (consume).** Why: applier owns index mutation. State: consumed. Risk: lag spike → freshness slips past 5s → alarm on consumer lag.
313	4. **Applier → Live Geo-Index (upsert, idempotent by ts).** Why: in-memory is the only thing fast enough. State: entity's current cell updated; if it crossed a boundary it's removed from the old cell. Risk: out-of-order ping → dropped by ts check.
314	5–6. **Stream → Sampler → History (5%).** Why: cheap analytics trail. State: durable sample written. Risk: sampler down → we lose *history* only, live path unaffected.
315	
316	> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "The 202 is the whole point — we acknowledge fast and apply async, because synchronously durably writing 750K pings a second would bankrupt us."
317	
318	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Location updates are the firehose, so the design is all about absorbing them cheaply. Ingest terminates the connection and acks with a 202, the ping rides a Kafka topic partitioned by cell, and an applier upserts it into the in-memory Redis index — dropping anything out of order by timestamp. We sample a small fraction to durable history and throw the rest away. The trade-off I accept is that an ack doesn't mean durable — but for live location that's exactly right, the next ping is four seconds away anyway."

320	### 6.3 — Register / update a static place (FR4)
321	
322	Low volume, but it must reach both the geo-index and the ranking store consistently.
323	
324	**Architecture decisions**
325	
326	| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
327	|---|---|---|---|---|
328	| Place Service | Service handling business CRUD, writes to durable DB | Static places need a durable source of truth (the DB) | Not writing directly to the in-memory index — it's volatile, places must survive restarts | Index lags the DB by CDC latency (seconds) |
329	| Places DB | Durable relational/document store, source of truth for places | ACID for a clean business record; low write volume | Not Redis — places are durable, not ephemeral | An extra propagation hop into the index |
330	| CDC Pipeline | Tails the DB change-log and emits "place changed" events | Keeps geo-index + ranking + cache in sync without dual-writes | Not dual-writing from the app — risks half-applied writes | Eventual: index trails DB by seconds |
331	
332	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
333	> **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — a stream of "row X changed" events read off the database's write-ahead log. Here it propagates place edits into the index and cache without the app dual-writing.
334	> **Source of truth** — the one authoritative copy of the data. Here the Places DB is authoritative; the index is a derived, rebuildable view.

362	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Static places are the easy write — low volume but they must be durable, so they land in a real database that's the source of truth, and a change-data-capture stream fans each edit out to the geo-index, the ranking store, and the cache. Two indexes — one durable for places, one in-memory for moving dots — is the spine everything hangs off."

406	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end. The richest places to go deep are the geo-index choice itself, the hot dense-city cell, and the moving-entity update path. Which do you want first — or is read-ranking more interesting to you?"

410	## 7. Data Model & Storage
411	
412	### PART A — Entity table
413	
414	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key) | Chosen store | Partition key | Consistency |
415	|---|---|---|---|---|
416	| Place | **id (PK)**, lat, lng, cell_id, category, rating, hours, version | Places DB (Postgres) | id (hash) | Strong on write, eventual to index |
417	| Geo-Index entry | **cell_id (shard key)**, member=entity_id, score=geohash/coords | Redis (sorted sets) per cell shard | cell_id | Eventual (rebuildable) |
418	| Live position | **entity_id (PK)**, lat, lng, ts, current_cell | Redis in-memory (live index) | current_cell | Last-writer-wins by ts |
419	| Ranking record | **entity_id (PK)**, rating, popularity, hours, open_now | Redis / KV cache, fed by CDC | entity_id (hash) | Eventual |
420	| Location history | **(entity_id, ts) (PK)** | Cassandra / object store (cold) | entity_id | Eventual, lossy sample |
421	
422	### PART B — Storage decision cards
423	
424	**🗄️ Redis (sorted sets) — used for: Geo-Index entries + Live positions**
425	1. **What it is:** an in-memory data store; its sorted-set type keeps members ordered by a numeric score, and it has native geo commands (GEOADD/GEOSEARCH) built on geohash scoring.
426	2. **Why — the access pattern that forces it:** we need 750K position upserts/s and millisecond cell scans; only RAM sustains that write rate, and Redis's geo sorted-set *is* a ready-made per-cell geo-index.
427	3. **Considered & rejected:** **PostGIS** (Postgres spatial) — excellent spatial queries but disk-bound, can't take the firehose. **Elasticsearch geo** — great for complex geo+text queries but heavier and slower to update under a firehose. **DynamoDB** — needs an app-side geohash scheme and lacks native expanding-ring scans.
428	4. **Trade-off:** volatile — on node loss we rebuild the live index from the Kafka stream (seconds), and the place index from CDC.
429	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "For the live, moving data I'm using Redis sorted sets, because it's in-memory and has native geo commands, so it eats the write firehose and answers cell scans in milliseconds. I looked at PostGIS — beautiful for spatial queries but it's on disk and would die under three-quarters of a million writes a second. The cost is it's volatile, but I can rebuild it from the Kafka log in seconds."
430	
431	**🗄️ Postgres — used for: Place (source of truth)**
432	1. **What it is:** a relational database that stores places in rows with strict schema and ACID transactions.
433	2. **Why:** static places are durable business records that must survive restarts and support clean edits with versioning; volume is low.
434	3. **Considered & rejected:** **Redis** — volatile, wrong for a source of truth. **MongoDB** — fine, but Postgres + PostGIS gives us a backup spatial query path for free if Redis is cold.
435	4. **Trade-off:** an extra CDC hop to propagate edits into the index; we accept seconds of lag.
436	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Places themselves live in Postgres as the durable source of truth, and CDC fans changes into the indexes. I'm not putting the authoritative place record in Redis because that data has to survive a restart."
437	
438	**🗄️ Kafka — used for: Update Stream (location firehose)**
439	1. **What it is:** a durable append-only log that buffers events and lets consumers read them in order at their own pace.
440	2. **Why:** it decouples the spiky 750K/s ingest from index apply, preserves per-cell ordering, and gives us a replay log to rebuild the volatile index.
441	3. **Considered & rejected:** **Direct writes** — couple ingest to index uptime; an index hiccup drops pings. **SQS** — no ordering or per-key partitioning.
442	4. **Trade-off:** adds 1-2s pipeline latency — within the 5s freshness budget.
443	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Kafka sits between ingest and the index as a shock absorber and a replay log — if the index restarts, I replay the stream to rebuild it."
444	
445	**🗄️ Cassandra / object store — used for: Location History (cold, sampled)**
446	1. **What it is:** a write-optimized distributed store for append-heavy, rarely-read history.
447	2. **Why:** even a 5% sample is high write volume; Cassandra's LSM-tree *(log-structured merge tree — buffers writes in memory and flushes sorted batches to disk, so writes are cheap)* is built for that.
448	3. **Considered & rejected:** **Postgres** — would choke on the append rate. **Storing all pings** — terabytes/day for negligible value.
449	4. **Trade-off:** lossy, eventually-consistent history — fine, it's analytics.
450	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "History is a sampled trail in Cassandra — write-cheap and we never need it on the hot path."
451	
452	### PART C — Per-operation consistency
453	
454	| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why |
455	|---|---|---|---|
456	| Search nearby (display) | Redis indexes | Eventual | A list of nearby things is a snapshot; staleness of seconds is invisible |
457	| Location upsert | Live index | Last-writer-wins by ts | Only the newest position matters; older pings are noise |
458	| Read-your-own-location | Live index | Read-after-write for self | A driver must see their own pin move (mitigation in §8) |
459	| Place edit | Postgres | Strong | Business record is authoritative |
460	| Place visible in search | Geo-Index | Eventual (CDC lag) | A new place appearing a few seconds late is fine |
461	
462	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The spine is two Redis index families — sorted sets for both the static geo-index and the live moving positions — because in-memory is the only thing that takes the write rate and answers cell scans in milliseconds. Postgres is the durable source of truth for places, Kafka is the shock absorber and replay log for the firehose, and Cassandra holds a sampled history trail. I considered PostGIS as the primary index and rejected it — gorgeous spatial queries, but disk-bound and it would die under the firehose — though I keep Postgres around as a cold backup spatial path. Almost everything is eventually consistent because a nearby list is just a snapshot; the one exception is a driver reading their own location."

466	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
467	
468	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two invariants — "don't scan the planet" (read) and "absorb the firehose" (write) — and reason forward from whichever the question touches. If pushed on a tech you don't know, reason from first principles: "its job here is X, so I'd expect…"
469	
470	### How do we choose and query the geo-index?
471	
472	**#### Bad: lat/lng columns + bounding-box SQL**
473	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** store every point's latitude and longitude and ask the database "give me everything between these four numbers."
474	- **Approach:** `WHERE lat BETWEEN a AND b AND lng BETWEEN c AND d`, then filter by exact distance.
475	- **Why people try it:** it's the obvious first thing and works for thousands of rows.
476	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** no spatial pruning — even with two B-tree indexes the DB intersects two huge ranges and scans millions of rows per query. At 50M points and 6.9K reads/s it's a full-table-ish scan every query; p99 blows past seconds.
477	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** the moment N is large, you need an index where *physical nearness maps to key nearness*.
478	
479	**#### Good: Geohash prefix index**
480	- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** range scans don't prune space; we need a 1-D key where neighbours are nearby.
481	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** squash the 2-D map into a single string where things that are close share a prefix, then a "nearby" search is just a prefix match.
482	- **Approach:** encode (lat,lng) → geohash *(a base-32 string; longer = more precise; shared prefix = physical nearness)*. Index by prefix; a radius query computes the covering prefixes and does prefix scans on the user's cell plus its 8 neighbours.
483	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** the **edge problem** — two points right across a cell boundary share *no* prefix despite being 10m apart, so you must always query 8 neighbour cells, and near the equator/poles cell sizes distort. Dense cells (a city) overflow into one huge prefix bucket → hot key.
484	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** uneven cell sizes + edge handling + dense-cell overflow push toward a hierarchical, variable-resolution index.
485	
486	```mermaid
487	flowchart TD
488	    Q[Query latlng radius]:::svc
489	    Q -->|"1 encode geohash"| H[Geohash prefix]:::svc
490	    H -->|"2 compute 8 neighbours"| N[Center plus neighbours]:::svc
491	    N -->|"3 prefix scan each"| GI[(Geohash buckets)]:::store
492	    GI -->|"4 exact distance filter"| R[Ranked results]:::svc
493	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
494	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
495	```
496	
497	**#### Great: S2 / Quadtree variable-resolution cells**
498	- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** fixed-size geohash cells overflow in cities and waste space in deserts, and the edge problem needs constant neighbour-stitching.
499	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** lay a grid over the Earth that automatically uses *small* squares where it's crowded and *big* squares where it's empty, so every square holds about the same number of things.
500	- **Approach:** use **S2** (maps the sphere to a cube then a Hilbert space-filling curve, giving cells at 30 levels with near-uniform area and excellent neighbour-finding) or a **quadtree** (recursively split a cell into 4 when it exceeds a capacity threshold). A radius query picks the right *level* for the radius, gets the covering cells, scans them. Dense cells are subdivided so no single cell is a giant bucket.
501	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** with fixed cells, a dense city cell could hold ~4% of 3M moving entities = 120K entities in one bucket — every scan reads them all. Variable resolution caps a cell at, say, ~1K entities, so a scan reads ~1K not 120K — a 100x cut in scan cost.
502	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
503	
504	| Scenario | Behaviour |
505	|---|---|
506	| Point on cell edge | S2 neighbour-cover includes adjacent cell; exact haversine filter trims |
507	| Dense city | Cell auto-subdivides; bounded entities per cell |
508	| Sparse desert | Coarse cell; one scan covers a huge area cheaply |
509	| Radius spans many cells | Pick coarser level; cover with fewer larger cells |
510	
511	```mermaid
512	flowchart TD
513	    Q[Query latlng radius]:::svc
514	    Q -->|"1 pick S2 level for radius"| L[S2 level]:::svc
515	    L -->|"2 covering cells"| C[Cell cover set]:::svc
516	    C -->|"3 scan bounded cells"| S2I[(S2 cell index)]:::store
517	    S2I -->|"4 subdivide if dense"| Sub[(Sub-cells)]:::store
518	    S2I -->|"5 exact filter and rank"| R[Ranked results]:::svc
519	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
520	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
521	```
522	
523	> 🎙️ **Script:** "I'd pick S2 cells. The naive lat-lng-between query scans millions of rows because range scans don't prune space. Geohash is the first real fix — it squashes the map into a prefix string so nearby things share a prefix — but it has the cell-edge problem and dense cities overflow into one giant bucket. S2 solves both: it's a variable-resolution grid, so a crowded city subdivides into small cells that each hold a bounded number of points, and its neighbour-finding is clean. Concretely, that turns a 120-thousand-entity city scan into a one-thousand-entity scan."
524	
525	> 🧠 **If they ask "geohash vs S2 — does it matter?":** "For pure radius on uniform data, geohash is simpler and fine. S2 earns its complexity exactly when density is skewed and you need variable resolution — which is our hot-city case."
526	
527	### How do we handle the hot dense-city cell? (the hot-key problem)
528	
529	**#### Bad: one cell = one shard**
530	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** put each map square on one machine.
531	- **Approach:** shard the index by cell_id; the cell owns all its entities on one node.
532	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** a dense city cell carries **~30K ops/s today** (mostly the moving-entity writes), and a single shard tops out **~50K/s**. We're at ~0.6x — one bad event (NYE, a surge) crosses 50K and that whole metro's search and updates stall. It's a single point of failure for the city.
533	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** any cell approaching the shard ceiling must be split.
534	
535	**#### Good: subdivide hot cells + read replicas**
536	- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** a fixed cell-to-shard mapping can't relieve a single overloaded cell.
537	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when a square gets too busy, cut it into four smaller squares on different machines, and add read-only copies for the search traffic.
538	- **Approach:** detect a cell crossing a QPS/entity threshold and **subdivide** it (S2 children) across shards; add **read replicas** so search reads spread out while writes go to the primary.
539	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** subdivision is reactive — there's a window during a sudden surge before the split lands. Replicas help reads but the *write* firehose still concentrates on the cell's primary.
540	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** need to split *writes* and react *before* overload.
541	
542	```mermaid
543	flowchart TD
544	    Hot[Hot city cell]:::store
545	    Hot -->|"1 split into children"| C1[(Sub-cell A)]:::store
546	    Hot -->|"1 split into children"| C2[(Sub-cell B)]:::store
547	    C1 -->|"2 read replica"| R1[(Replica A)]:::store
548	    Search[Search Service]:::svc -->|"3 reads to replicas"| R1
549	    Applier[Index Applier]:::svc -->|"4 writes to primary"| C1
550	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
551	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
552	```
553	
554	**#### Great: predictive sub-celling + write-side cell sharding + query cache**
555	- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** reactive splitting lags surges and doesn't relieve write concentration.
556	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** learn which cities get hot when (lunch, commute, events), pre-split them ahead of time, spread their writes across machines, and cache the popular searches so most reads never touch the shard at all.
557	- **Approach:** (1) a **heat monitor** tracks per-cell QPS and *predictively* pre-subdivides known-hot cells before peak; (2) **write-side sharding** — within a hot cell, hash entities to N sub-shards so 30K writes/s splits to ~7.5K/s each; (3) the **query cache** absorbs ~80% of the *read* side at the cell level. Reads were always the small part here, so caching plus sub-celling keeps every shard under ceiling.
558	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** hot cell at 30K/s ÷ 4 sub-shards = ~7.5K/s each, comfortably under the 50K ceiling with 6x headroom for surges. Reads (0.3K/s of the 30K) are negligible once cached.
559	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
560	
561	| Scenario | Behaviour |
562	|---|---|
563	| Sudden surge past 50K | Pre-split already in place; if not, reactive split + shed lowest-priority pings |
564	| One sub-shard dies | Its entities re-bucket to siblings; bounded reload from Kafka |
565	| Cache cold (event start) | Reads fall to replicas; still under ceiling because reads are tiny |
566	| Cell cools down | Merge sub-cells back to save shards (cost) |
567	
568	```mermaid
569	flowchart TD
570	    Heat[Heat Monitor]:::svc
571	    Heat -->|"1 predict hot cell"| Pre[Pre-split planner]:::svc
572	    Pre -->|"2 create N sub-shards"| SS[(Sub-shards 1 to N)]:::store
573	    Applier[Index Applier]:::svc -->|"3 hash writes across N"| SS
574	    Search[Search Service]:::svc -->|"4 read cache first"| Cache[(Query Cache)]:::store
575	    Cache -.->|"5 miss to sub-shards"| SS
576	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
577	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
578	```
579	
580	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The hot key here is a dense city cell — and the surprise is it's hot from *writes*, not reads, because that's where the drivers physically are. One cell on one shard is about thirty thousand ops a second against a fifty-thousand ceiling, so a surge tips it over and that whole metro stalls. The fix has three parts: predictively pre-split known-hot cities before peak so we're not reacting late, hash the writes within a hot cell across several sub-shards so each takes a quarter of the load, and let the query cache absorb the read side. That keeps every shard at maybe seven and a half thousand a second with plenty of headroom."
581	
582	> 🧠 **If they ask "how do you detect hot before it's too late?":** "A rolling per-cell QPS counter with a threshold alarm, plus a calendar/historical model for predictable peaks like lunch and weekend nights — pre-split on schedule, reactive-split as backstop."
583	
584	### How do we keep moving entities fresh without melting the write path?
585	
586	**#### Bad: write every ping straight to the DB**
587	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every time a driver moves, save it to the database immediately.
588	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** 750K durable disk writes/s is unaffordable and the DB connection pool exhausts in seconds; this is the classic Uber trap — batch thinking or naive durable writes where real-time is needed.
589	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** must decouple ack from durability.
590	
591	**#### Good: in-memory index + async durability**
592	- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** synchronous durable writes can't take the rate.
593	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep the live positions in memory, ack instantly, and only trickle a sample to disk.
594	- **Approach:** ack 202, apply to in-memory index, sample 5% to history. Re-bucket only on **cell crossing**, not every ping (ignore sub-cell GPS jitter).
595	- ⚠️ **What breaks:** on a node restart the in-memory index is empty → a freshness gap; and a driver right after moving might read their *own* stale pin (read-your-own-writes).
596	- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** need fast rebuild + read-your-own-writes.
597	
598	```mermaid
599	flowchart TD
600	    Ping[Location ping]:::client
601	    Ping -->|"1 ack 202"| Mem[(In-memory index)]:::store
602	    Mem -->|"2 rebucket on cell cross"| Cell[(New cell)]:::store
603	    Mem -.->|"3 sample to history"| Hist[(History)]:::store
604	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
605	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
606	```
607	
608	**#### Great: Kafka replay + read-your-own-writes token**
609	- ↩️ **Previous got wrong:** no rebuild story and no self-consistency.
610	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep a replay log so a restarted node refills its memory in seconds, and stamp each ack with a version so a client always sees at least its own latest move.
611	- **Approach:** Kafka is the replay log partitioned by cell — a restarted applier replays recent offsets to rebuild its cells in seconds. Each ack returns a `version`/`ts`; the entity's own client sends it back on the next read, and the index serves *at least* that version (read-your-own-writes for self). Out-of-order pings dropped by ts.
612	- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** a restarted shard owning ~1K entities replays a few seconds of its cell partitions — rebuild well under the 5s freshness budget.
613	- ✅ **Failure matrix:**
614	
615	| Scenario | Behaviour |
616	|---|---|
617	| Applier restart | Replay Kafka offsets → cells rebuilt in seconds |
618	| Out-of-order ping | Dropped by ts (idempotent) |
619	| Driver reads own pin | version token guarantees they see their latest move |
620	| Kafka lag spike | Freshness slips; consumer-lag alarm; shed sampling first |
621	
622	```mermaid
623	flowchart TD
624	    Ping[Location ping]:::client
625	    Ping -->|"1 publish by cell"| K[[Kafka by cell]]:::async
626	    K -->|"2 apply with ts"| Mem[(In-memory index)]:::store
627	    K -.->|"3 replay on restart"| Mem
628	    Mem -->|"4 ack version token"| Ping
629	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
630	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
631	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
632	```
633	
634	> 🎙️ **Script:** "For moving entities the rule is never write a ping straight to disk — at three-quarters of a million a second the database dies. So I ack a 202, apply to the in-memory index, and only re-bucket when the entity crosses a cell boundary, ignoring GPS jitter. Kafka sits underneath as a replay log partitioned by cell, so if a node restarts it refills its memory from the last few seconds of the log, well inside our five-second freshness window. And I hand back a version token on each ack so a driver always sees at least their own latest position."
635	
636	> 🧠 **If they ask "what about exactly-once?":** "I don't need it — last-writer-wins by timestamp is idempotent, so an at-least-once stream replaying a ping is harmless; only the newest position survives."
637

639	
640	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
641	
642	### 9A — Availability targets
643	
644	- **Read path: four nines (99.99%) ≈ 52 minutes downtime/year.** Mechanisms: stateless Search Service behind a load balancer across **multi-AZ** *(multiple data centres in one region, so one building's outage doesn't take the service down)*; read replicas of each index; the query cache serves even if a shard blips.
645	- **Write firehose: three-and-a-half nines on the path, but degrades softly.** A dropped ping is recovered by the next ping ~4s later, so we tolerate brief firehose loss without user-visible breakage.
646	- **Place writes: three nines is plenty** — low volume, retriable.
647	
648	### 9B — Per-dependency failure table
649	
650	| Component | What breaks | Graceful degradation — what the USER sees | Recovery |
651	|---|---|---|---|
652	| Query Cache (Redis) | Cached pages gone | Reads fall through to index replicas; slightly slower, fully correct | Cache refills on traffic |
653	| Geo-Index shard | One cell's static places unreadable | That metro's *place* search degrades; live dots still work | Promote replica; rebuild from CDC |
654	| Live index shard | One cell's moving dots stale | "No drivers nearby" briefly in that cell | Replay Kafka offsets, seconds |
655	| Kafka | Firehose buffering stops | Live dots freeze at last position; reads still return them | Buffer at ingest, then shed with backpressure |
656	| Places DB | Can't edit/create places | Search unaffected (reads from index); edits queued/rejected | Failover to replica; retries |
657	| Index Applier | Updates stop applying | Freshness slips past 5s in affected cells | Consumer rebalances, replays |
658	
659	### 9C — RPO / RTO per data class
660	
661	> **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we're OK losing if it crashes right now. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back.
662	
663	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
664	|---|---|---|---|
665	| Places (source of truth) | ~0 (sync-replicated commit) | < 1 min | Postgres multi-AZ failover |
666	| Live positions | ~seconds (acceptable; rebuilt from stream) | < 10s | Kafka replay into memory |
667	| Geo-Index (derived) | n/a — rebuildable | minutes | Rebuild from Postgres CDC |
668	| Location history | minutes (lossy sample anyway) | n/a | Cassandra replication |
669	
670	### 9D — Cost (rough monthly order-of-magnitude)
671	
672	Top 3 drivers (from `capacity.py`):
673	1. **Location-ingest fleet (~$30K/mo)** — the firehose needs ~38 stateless nodes to terminate 750K writes/s. This is the biggest line item — because it runs always-on at high volume. *Biggest optimization: raise the ping interval from 4s → 8s when an entity is stationary/idle, roughly halving write volume and this cost.*
674	2. **Hot in-memory cache + live index RAM (~$5K/mo)** — RAM is pricier per byte but the dataset is tiny (~0.3GB live), so this stays small.
675	3. **Geo-index read replicas (~$3K/mo)** — reads are small (6.9K/s), so a couple of replicas suffice.
676	
677	Order-of-magnitude total: **~$40K/mo — low six figures annually.** Notably the *database* is NOT the cost driver; the always-on write firehose is.
678	
679	> 🎙️ **Script:** "When the cache dies, search just gets a hair slower but stays correct. When a live-index shard dies, that one city's moving dots go briefly stale and we replay Kafka to refill them in seconds. When Kafka itself backs up, dots freeze at their last position rather than vanishing — the user still sees something reasonable. The thing that would actually hurt is the Places DB, so that's the one we keep at RPO near zero. And the surprise on cost is that the always-on location-ingest fleet, not the database, is the biggest bill — so the cheapest win is slowing the ping rate for idle entities."
680

683	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
684	
685	**🔀 Decision: In-memory Redis geo-index vs disk-based PostGIS**
686	1. **Chose & why:** Redis sorted sets — only RAM sustains 750K updates/s with ms scans.
687	2. **Gave up:** durability and rich spatial SQL; the index is volatile.
688	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Like a whiteboard versus a filing cabinet — the whiteboard is instant but you must be able to redraw it from notes if it's wiped."
689	4. **Reverses when:** if entities become mostly static (Yelp-only) and the write rate collapses, PostGIS becomes the simpler single-store answer — no rebuild machinery.
690	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I went in-memory because this is a write firehose; if it were static places only, I'd just use PostGIS and skip all the rebuild plumbing."
691	
692	**🔀 Decision: 202-async location writes vs synchronous durable writes**
693	1. **Chose & why:** ack fast, apply async — the only affordable way to take the firehose.
694	2. **Gave up:** an ack does not guarantee durability.
695	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We say 'got it' before it's filed, because the next update is four seconds away anyway."
696	4. **Reverses when:** if a single missed location had safety/legal consequences (e.g. emergency dispatch), I'd add synchronous durability for that entity class.
697	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "For live dots, losing one ping is harmless — the next one fixes it — so I trade durability for throughput."
698	
699	**🔀 Decision: S2 variable-resolution cells vs fixed geohash**
700	1. **Chose & why:** variable resolution caps entities-per-cell so dense cities don't overflow.
701	2. **Gave up:** more complexity than a geohash prefix.
702	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "Smart grid that uses small squares in crowded places and big ones in empty places."
703	4. **Reverses when:** if density were uniform (e.g. a sensor grid), geohash's simplicity wins.
704	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "S2 earns its complexity precisely because our load is skewed to cities; on uniform data I'd use plain geohash."
705	
706	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'm least dogmatic about: in-memory over PostGIS, which I'd flip the instant this became a static-only Yelp; 202-async writes over durable, which I'd flip for a safety-critical entity class like emergency dispatch; and S2 over geohash, which only earns its keep because our traffic clusters in cities. Each one traces back to the same root: this is a write-heavy, geographically-skewed, eventually-consistent system."
707	
708	---
709	
710	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
711	
712	**1) Core / hardest part**
713	
714	**❓ "How does a radius query avoid scanning all 50M points?"**
715	- **Mechanism:** the geo-index maps each point to an S2 cell; the query computes the small set of cells covering the circle (the cell *cover*), scans only those, then applies an exact haversine filter and sort.
716	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We only look at the few map squares around you, not the whole map."
717	- 💬 "Cell-cover the radius, scan ~9 cells, then exact-distance the short list."
718	
719	**❓ "Geohash, quadtree, or S2 — what and why?"**
720	- **Mechanism:** geohash is a simple prefix string (edge problem, fixed size); quadtree subdivides on density; S2 gives variable-resolution near-uniform-area cells with clean neighbours. We pick S2 for skewed density.
721	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "S2 is a smart grid that shrinks squares in busy areas — so cities don't overflow."
722	- 💬 "S2, because our density is lopsided and we need variable resolution."
723	
724	**2) Failure**
725	
726	**❓ "What if Redis (the live index) crashes?"**
727	- **Mechanism:** the live index is volatile but Kafka is the replay log partitioned by cell; a restarted node replays recent offsets and rebuilds its cells in seconds, inside the 5s freshness budget.
728	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We redraw the live map from the recent log — takes seconds."
729	- 💬 "Volatile is fine — Kafka replays the index back in seconds."
730	
731	**❓ "What if Kafka backs up?"**
732	- **Mechanism:** ingest buffers briefly then applies backpressure; live dots freeze at last known position rather than disappearing, and consumer-lag alarms fire.
733	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Dots stop moving for a bit, but they don't vanish."
734	- 💬 "Freshness degrades gracefully; we freeze, not drop."
735	
736	**3) Scale / hot-spot**
737	
738	**❓ "New Year's Eve in Times Square — what happens?"**
739	- **Mechanism:** that cell crosses the ~50K/s shard ceiling, so the heat monitor (predictively, ideally pre-split) subdivides it into sub-shards and hashes writes across them; the query cache absorbs reads.
740	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We cut the busy square into smaller squares on more machines, ahead of time."
741	- 💬 "Pre-split hot cells, hash writes across sub-shards, cache the reads."
742	
743	**❓ "Reads or writes are the bottleneck here?"**
744	- **Mechanism:** writes — 750K/s vs ~6.9K/s reads, about 108:1; the design spends its budget on the firehose, not caching.
745	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "It's a writing problem, not a reading problem."
746	- 💬 "Hundred-to-one writes — this is a firehose, not a search engine."
747	
748	**4) Consistency / correctness**
749	
750	**❓ "Can a driver see their own pin lag behind?"**
751	- **Mechanism:** each ack returns a version/ts token; the driver's next read sends it back and the index serves at least that version — read-your-own-writes for self.
752	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You always see at least your own latest move."
753	- 💬 "Version token guarantees self-consistency."
754	
755	**❓ "Out-of-order GPS pings — won't the pin jump backwards?"**
756	- **Mechanism:** every update carries a ts; the index keeps last-writer-wins by ts and drops anything older, so it's idempotent and order-immune.
757	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We ignore any update older than what we already have."
758	- 💬 "Last-writer-wins by timestamp — stale pings are dropped."
759	
760	**5) Security**
761	
762	**❓ "Can someone move another user's pin or spoof location?"**
763	- **Mechanism:** entity_id comes from the JWT, never the body, so you can only move your own pin; and we sanity-check pings against plausible speed/recent positions to flag teleporting.
764	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You can only move yourself, and we catch impossible jumps."
765	- 💬 "Identity from the token, plus a speed-plausibility check."
766	
767	**❓ "Privacy of live locations?"**
768	- **Mechanism:** results return coarse, ranked entities, not raw continuous tracks; history is a sampled, access-controlled, retention-limited trail (PII).
769	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We don't hand out anyone's full movement history."
770	- 💬 "Coarse results, sampled history, retention limits."
771	
772	**6) Cost**
773	
774	**❓ "What's the most expensive part?"**
775	- **Mechanism:** the always-on location-ingest fleet (~$30K/mo of ~$40K) — high-volume writes run 24/7; the DB is not the driver.
776	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Paying to catch millions of location pings forever is the big bill."
777	- 💬 "The write firehose ingest fleet, not the database."
778	
779	**❓ "How would you cut cost the most?"**
780	- **Mechanism:** adaptive ping rate — slow stationary/idle entities from 4s to 8s+, roughly halving write volume and the ingest fleet.
781	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Ping less often when you're not moving."
782	- 💬 "Adaptive ping interval — biggest single lever."
783	
784	**7) Extensibility**
785	
786	**❓ "How would you add 'show me drivers along my route', not just a radius?"**
787	- **Mechanism:** a route is a polyline; cover it with S2 cells (a thicker corridor of cells) instead of a circle, then the same scan-hydrate-rank path applies.
788	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Light up the squares along your path instead of around a point."
789	- 💬 "Cell-cover the route corridor; reuse the read path."
790	
791	**❓ "How would you add ranked recommendations (not just distance)?"**
792	- **Mechanism:** the ranking store already holds rating/popularity; we plug a scoring function (or an offline ML model's scores) into the final sort after the geo-filter — geo stays the candidate generator, ranking is pluggable.
793	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Geo finds the candidates; a separate scorer orders them."
794	- 💬 "Geo is candidate-gen, ranking is a pluggable scorer on top."
795	
796	**❓ "Multi-region — users in Europe and the US?"**
797	- **Mechanism:** geo data is naturally partitioned by location, so route each user to the region owning their cells; cross-region is rare (you search near where you are), so single-write-region per geo with async global metadata replication.
798	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Your data lives where you are; you rarely search across an ocean."
799	- 💬 "Partition by geography — region owns its cells, almost no cross-region."
800	
801	> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "It's a service that, given where you are, returns the nearby things — places or moving people — ranked, fast. The design splits into two halves. The read half cell-covers your radius with an S2 spatial index so we touch a handful of map cells instead of fifty million points, reads the static places from a durable-backed index and the moving dots from an in-memory one, hydrates ratings, does an exact distance sort, and caches the popular city queries. The write half is the harder part — three quarters of a million location pings a second, a hundred-to-one over reads — and the one mechanism that makes it affordable is acking fast with a 202 and applying asynchronously to an in-memory Redis index buffered behind Kafka, which doubles as the replay log to rebuild that volatile index in seconds. The two halves connect through the geo-cell: it's both the read-time cover unit and the write-time shard key, and the hot dense-city cell is the bottleneck we relieve by predictively sub-splitting it across shards. Every hard decision traces back to one principle — this is a write-heavy, geographically-skewed, eventually-consistent system, so we spend the budget on the firehose and let the reads cache away."
802

content.text:
The read-your-own-writes mechanism has a subtle technical hole worth flagging: the doc says "the index serves *at least* that version" — but the write path is async (202, applied via Kafka). If the client's read arrives at the in-memory index before the Kafka pipeline has applied that version (the 1-2s pipeline latency the doc itself cites at line 276/442), the index simply won't have that version yet. The doc says "serves at least that version" without explaining how the read blocks/waits or where the unapplied write is found. A truly sound RYOW with an async apply pipeline needs either: read-through to the client's own last-known position cached at ingest, or a read that waits for the version to land. The doc asserts the guarantee but the async pipeline can't trivially honor "serve at least version V" if V isn't applied yet. This is a real hand-wave on the consistency mechanism — MAJOR, since RYOW/consistency was explicitly called out in the review criteria.
